elaboration.definition: Reproductive structure of seed plants containing an embryo, a nutrient reserve (endosperm), and a protective seed coat (testa).
elaboration.examples: Bean, corn kernel, acorn, almond, coconut.
elaboration.notes: Seeds can remain viable for years or even centuries in dormancy. The record belongs to lotus seeds found in a dried lake bed in China and germinated after ~1300 years.

elaboration.definition: The process by which a seed embryo resumes metabolic activity, breaks dormancy, and develops into a seedling.
elaboration.examples: A bean sprouting after watering; wheat germinating in a field within 4–7 days.
elaboration.notes: Germination requires water, adequate temperature, and in some species light or darkness. Dormancy is an evolutionary mechanism that prevents germination under unfavorable conditions.

elaboration.definition: A multicellular eukaryotic autotroph belonging to the kingdom Plantae, capable of producing energy through photosynthesis.
elaboration.examples: Oak tree, tomato plant, cactus, fern, moss.
elaboration.notes: Plants form the base of most terrestrial food webs and produce roughly 70% of atmospheric oxygen. There are an estimated 400,000 known plant species.

elaboration.definition: The typically underground vegetative organ that anchors the plant in the soil and absorbs water and dissolved minerals.
elaboration.examples: Taproot of a carrot; fibrous roots of grass; aerial roots of vanilla orchid.
elaboration.notes: Some roots serve special functions: nutrient storage (beet, sweet potato), aerial support (mangrove), or mycorrhizal symbiosis with fungi to extend nutrient uptake.

elaboration.definition: The main axis of a plant that supports leaves, flowers, and fruit while transporting raw sap (xylem) and elaborated sap (phloem) between roots and leaves.
elaboration.examples: Tree trunk, rose stalk, bamboo cane, strawberry stolon.
elaboration.notes: Stems can be woody (trees, shrubs) or herbaceous. Modified stems include tubers (potato), rhizomes (ginger), and bulbs (onion).

elaboration.definition: The flattened lateral organ of vascular plants, the primary site of photosynthesis and gas exchange through stomata.
elaboration.examples: Lobed oak leaf, pine needle, palmate maple leaf, lanceolate olive leaf.
elaboration.notes: Leaf morphology (shape, margin, venation) is a key diagnostic character for plant identification. Leaves are also the primary site of transpiration, driving water uptake from roots.

elaboration.definition: The reproductive structure of angiosperms, composed of the calyx (sepals), corolla (petals), androecium (stamens), and gynoecium (pistil).
elaboration.examples: Rose, sunflower, tulip, orchid, apple blossom.
elaboration.notes: Colored petals, fragrances, and nectar are evolutionary adaptations to attract specific pollinators. Some plants such as corn have reduced, petal-less flowers pollinated by wind.

elaboration.definition: The mature ovary of a flowering plant that encloses and protects the seeds and aids in their dispersal.
elaboration.examples: Apple, tomato, bean pod, cherry, coconut.
elaboration.notes: Botanically, a tomato, pepper, and zucchini are all fruits. Fleshy fruits attract animals that disperse seeds; dry fruits like dandelion use wind dispersal.

elaboration.definition: An inorganic molecule (H₂O) essential for plants: a reactant in photosynthesis, a medium for nutrient transport through xylem, and the driver of cell turgor.
elaboration.examples: Water absorbed by roots and transported in xylem; transpired through leaf stomata; photolyzed in the light reactions of photosynthesis.
elaboration.notes: Water makes up 70–95% of a plant's fresh weight. Water deficit triggers stomata closure, slowing photosynthesis and growth; prolonged drought leads to wilting and death.

elaboration.definition: Electromagnetic radiation from the Sun that provides the energy driving the light reactions of photosynthesis.
elaboration.examples: Red light (~660 nm) and blue light (~430 nm) absorbed by chlorophyll; green light reflected (giving leaves their color).
elaboration.notes: Plants have evolved responses to light including phototropism (growth toward light) and photoperiodism (regulation of flowering based on day length).

elaboration.definition: The uppermost layer of Earth's crust, composed of minerals, organic matter, water, air, and microorganisms, in which roots anchor and grow.
elaboration.examples: Clay soil (rich in minerals, poor drainage), sandy soil (good drainage, low nutrients), peat soil (high organic content, acidic).
elaboration.notes: Soil pH affects nutrient availability; most plants thrive between pH 6 and 7. Soil microorganisms (bacteria, fungi) are critical for nutrient cycling and plant health.

elaboration.definition: An atmospheric gas (CO₂) absorbed by leaves through stomata and used as the sole carbon source in photosynthesis.
elaboration.examples: CO₂ at ~420 ppm absorbed by corn leaves during the day; CO₂ released by all tissues during respiration.
elaboration.notes: Rising atmospheric CO₂ from human activity has a fertilizing effect on C3 plants but also drives climate change and ocean acidification.

elaboration.definition: The biochemical process by which plants convert light energy, CO₂, and water into glucose and oxygen using chlorophyll.
elaboration.examples: 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂. Occurs in the green leaves of any plant exposed to sunlight.
elaboration.notes: Photosynthesis has two stages: the light reactions (in thylakoids: producing ATP, NADPH, and O₂) and the Calvin cycle (in the stroma: fixing CO₂ into glucose).

elaboration.definition: Gas (O₂) released as a by-product of photosynthesis through the photolysis of water in the light reactions.
elaboration.examples: Bubbles of O₂ released by aquatic plants (e.g., Elodea) under light; O₂ produced by forests and phytoplankton.
elaboration.notes: Earth's oxygen-rich atmosphere arose ~2.4 billion years ago from photosynthetic cyanobacteria, in an event called the Great Oxidation Event.

elaboration.definition: A monosaccharide (C₆H₁₂O₆) produced by photosynthesis, the primary energy currency and carbon building block for plant biosynthesis.
elaboration.examples: Glucose stored in leaves; converted to sucrose for phloem transport; converted to fructose in sweet fruit pulp.
elaboration.notes: Excess glucose is polymerized into starch (energy storage in seeds and tubers) or cellulose (structural component of cell walls).

elaboration.definition: The green photosynthetic pigment in chloroplasts responsible for absorbing light energy to drive photosynthesis.
elaboration.examples: Chlorophyll a (primary, blue-green) and chlorophyll b (accessory, yellow-green) in higher plants; bacteriochlorophyll in photosynthetic bacteria.
elaboration.notes: Chlorophyll appears green because it reflects green light while absorbing mainly red and blue. In autumn, chlorophyll degrades, unmasking yellow and orange carotenoid pigments in deciduous leaves.

elaboration.definition: The transfer of pollen from an anther to a stigma of the same species, required for fertilization and seed production in angiosperms.
elaboration.examples: Bees carrying apple pollen; wind dispersing grass or hazel pollen; butterflies pollinating lavender.
elaboration.notes: Pollination can be entomophilous (insects), anemophilous (wind), ornithophilous (birds), or hydrophilous (water). About 87% of flowering plant species depend on animal pollinators.

elaboration.definition: The cellular metabolic process by which the plant oxidizes glucose to produce ATP, releasing CO₂ and water as by-products.
elaboration.examples: Nocturnal leaf respiration (when photosynthesis is inactive); intense respiration in germinating seeds.
elaboration.notes: Unlike photosynthesis, respiration occurs in all plant tissues at all times. Under good light conditions, photosynthesis produces far more O₂ than respiration consumes.

elaboration.definition: A plant that completes its entire life cycle (germination, growth, flowering, fruiting, death) within a single year or growing season.
elaboration.examples: Wheat, corn, sunflower, basil, petunia.
elaboration.notes: Annual plants survive winter only as seeds. Many of the world's most important crops are annuals, bred to produce large quantities of seeds or fruits rapidly.

elaboration.definition: A plant that lives for more than two years, flowering and fruiting repeatedly throughout its lifespan.
elaboration.examples: Oak tree, wild rose, lavender, peony, olive tree.
elaboration.notes: Woody perennials form secondary tissue (wood) and grow year after year. Herbaceous perennials may lose their aerial parts in winter and regrow from roots or rhizomes the following season.
